feed.title: PGM Lab
feed.subtitle: The Laboratory for Precision Genome Medicine (PGM Lab) at Yonsei University College of Medicine conducts translational research at the intersection of human genetics and precision medicine, integrating wet-lab and computational approaches to improve disease diagnosis and treatment.

entry.title: Welcome to Our First Internship Students! 🥳

link.title: Welcome to Our First Internship Students! 🥳

entry.content:
We are delighted to welcome two students selected for this year’s summer internship.
Over the coming months, they will take part in sequencing data generation and analysis spanning clinical genomics and long-read sequencing.

Sophia Sim
Johns Hopkins University, Pre-Med Student
Major: Molecular and Cellular Biology

Mandalmaa Bayasgalan
Yonsei University, Undergraduate Student
Major: Life Science and Biotechnology

We hope this internship offers both of them a valuable, hands-on introduction to genomics,
and we look forward to the fresh energy and new perspectives they bring to our work. 🌱
entry.summary: We are delighted to welcome two students selected for this year’s summer internship. Over the coming months, they will take part in sequencing data generation and analysis spanning clinical genomics and long-read sequencing. Sophia Sim Johns Hopkins University, Pre-Med Student Major: Molecular and Cellular Biology

entry.title: Our Lab T-Shirt 👕 is ready 👊👊
entry.content:
The lab now has the first version of its own T-shirt, and we could not be more excited to wear it!

“SEQUENCING LIFE” is our humorous slogan for everyday lab life.

Printed in crisp white on deep navy, the front lays out what we do every day as a five-step pipeline:

PIPETTE ADDICTED → GENOME SEQUENCING → DNA IS OUR CODE → LINUX POWERED → DATA NEVER SLEEPS,

complete with a pipette, a nanopore sequencer, a DNA double helix, the Linux penguin, and a humming server.

Below the icons, a progress bar reads

LOADING GENOMES… 99% — a wink at every analysis that is almost done but somehow always needs one more run. 😄

It carries the PGM Lab name and our motto, “Decoding the Genomes, Transforming Patient Care,” showing the playful design back to what we actually do every day.

I designed this 2026 edition of our lab T-shirt so we could carry a little of the joy of scientific discovery with us wherever we go.

I hope this t-shirt stays with each of you as a small token of the time we shared decoding genomes together.

A small thing, but a fun way to show some lab spirit — and a reminder that behind every variant call there is a team that loves this work. 🤣
entry.summary: The lab now has the first version of its own T-shirt, and we could not be more excited to wear it!

entry.title: Our Research Featured in Gangnam Severance Magazine “Always YOUNG” 📖

link.title: Our Research Featured in Gangnam Severance Magazine “Always YOUNG” 📖

entry.content:
Our research was featured in the RESEARCH INTERVIEW section of Gangnam Severance Hospital’s magazine “Always YOUNG” (2026, Vol.126), where our lab’s work on AI-based interpretation of genomic variants was introduced.

It is a great honor to see our research highlighted in the hospital magazine, and we hope it offers patients and their families a glimpse into the science working quietly behind their care. 🏥

👉 Read the full interview (Korean): Always YOUNG Vol.126
entry.summary: Our research was featured in the RESEARCH INTERVIEW section of Gangnam Severance Hospital’s magazine “Always YOUNG” (2026, Vol.126), where our lab’s work on AI-based interpretation of genomic variants was introduced.

entry.title: Thursday Lab Meeting — Allele-Specific Methylation Analysis in the Nanopore WGS dataset 🧬

link.title: Thursday Lab Meeting — Allele-Specific Methylation Analysis in the Nanopore WGS dataset 🧬

entry.content:
At this week’s Thursday lab meeting, Ayoub presented his ongoing analysis of allele-specific methylations (ASM) in 48 Korean genomes profiled by Oxford Nanopore long-read whole-genome sequencing.

Leveraging the CpG methylation signals natively available from ONT data, Ayoub walked us through his established pipeline for phasing methylation onto haplotypes and identifying loci with allelic imbalance across the cohort.

He shared new findings from the dataset, including novel ASM regions as well as validation of previously reported loci, illustrating how integrating methylation and haplotype information sharpens the resolution at which regulatory variation can be pinpointed in a population-scale Korean dataset.

Thanks Ayoub for the interesting presentation, and to everyone who joined the lively discussion afterward — looking forward to seeing where this work leads! 🚀
entry.summary: At this week’s Thursday lab meeting, Ayoub presented his ongoing analysis of allele-specific methylations (ASM) in 48 Korean genomes profiled by Oxford Nanopore long-read whole-genome sequencing.

entry.title: Our Lab at the 71st KSMGG Spring Conference! 🧬

link.title: Our Lab at the 71st KSMGG Spring Conference! 🧬

entry.content:
On April 23, 2026, our lab attended the 71st Spring Conference of the Korean Society of Medical Genetics and Genomics (KSMGG), held at the Baekbeom Kim Koo Memorial Hall in Seoul.

The program offered a rich overview of where medical genetics is heading — from prime editing-based functional variant evaluation and brain somatic mutations in psychiatric disease, to multi-omics approaches for metabolic complexity, multi-center WGS pipelines for ending the diagnostic odyssey in rare diseases, and AI-driven liquid biopsy and precision immuno-oncology.

We are especially proud that Dr. Yoon delivered a talk in Session II-B (Clinical Translation of Genomic Data: Bench-to-Bedside in Rare Disease Research), titled “From Sequence Extension to Disease: Integrative Functional Prediction of Stop-loss Variants,” building on our recently published work. Many thanks to the KSMGG organizing committee and to all the colleagues who joined us for the lively discussions — we came back energized and full of new ideas to carry into our ongoing work! 🚀
entry.summary: On April 23, 2026, our lab attended the 71st Spring Conference of the Korean Society of Medical Genetics and Genomics (KSMGG), held at the Baekbeom Kim Koo Memorial Hall in Seoul. The program offered a rich overview of where medical genetics is heading — from prime editing-based functional variant evaluation and brain somatic mutations in psychiatric disease, to multi-omics approaches for metabolic complexity, multi-center WGS pipelines for ending the diagnostic odyssey in rare diseases, and AI-driven liquid biopsy and precision immuno-oncology. We are especially proud that Dr. Yoon delivered a talk in Session II-B (Clinical Translation of Genomic Data: Bench-to-Bedside in Rare Disease Research), titled “From Sequence Extension to Disease: Integrative Functional Prediction of Stop-loss Variants,” building on our recently published work. Many thanks to the KSMGG organizing committee and to all the colleagues who joined us for the lively discussions — we came back energized and full of new ideas to carry into our ongoing work! 🚀

entry.title: Welcome to the new member, Jongung Song! 🎉

link.title: Welcome to the new member, Jongung Song! 🎉

entry.content:
We’re excited to have him join our lab and look forward to the impact his computational skills will bring to our research. We are especially excited about the contributions he will make through data science, machine learning, and computational analysis in our ongoing projects.

Welcome to the team, Jongung — we’re glad to have you with us! 🚀
entry.summary: We’re excited to have him join our lab and look forward to the impact his computational skills will bring to our research. We are especially excited about the contributions he will make through data science, machine learning, and computational analysis in our ongoing projects.

entry.title: Welcome to join our lab, Dr.Song!

link.title: Welcome to join our lab, Dr.Song!

entry.content:
We are delighted to welcome Dr. Moon-Young Song to our lab as our first postdoctoral researcher! 🎉

Dr. Song brings strong expertise in translational oncology and wet experiments, and we are excited to have her join the team. We look forward to the impactful research and new directions her work will bring to our lab.

Welcome aboard, Dr. Song!
entry.summary: We are delighted to welcome Dr. Moon-Young Song to our lab as our first postdoctoral researcher! 🎉

entry.title: TAILVAR paper is officially accepted for NAR publication!

link.title: TAILVAR paper is officially accepted for NAR publication!

entry.content:
We are excited to announce that the first paper from our lab has been officially accepted for publication in Nucleic Acids Research (NAR) 🎉

In this study, we introduce TAILVAR, a predictive framework that substantially improves the interpretation of stop-loss variants. Unlike previous in silico models, TAILVAR explicitly incorporates the biological characteristics of C-terminal extensions, providing both enhanced predictive performance and new insights into genome-wide stop codon usage and its functional impact.

Our results highlight that features of C-terminal extensions—long overlooked in variant interpretation—play a critical role in determining the functional impact of stop-loss variants. This work not only advances computational variant prediction but also deepens our understanding of protein biology beyond canonical termination.

Well done team, and we’re excited for what comes next.
entry.summary: We are excited to announce that the first paper from our lab has been officially accepted for publication in Nucleic Acids Research (NAR) 🎉

entry.title: Welcome AYOUB!
entry.content: We’re excited to welcome Ayoub EL Bakiallah to our lab as the first intern! 🎉 Welcome to the team, Ayoub!
entry.summary: We’re excited to welcome Ayoub EL Bakiallah to our lab as the first intern! 🎉 Welcome to the team, Ayoub!

entry.title: Homepage is open now!
entry.content:
I am happy to share the first post in the PGM Lab.

Our website is now available through github! Looking forward to post news and updates in our lab 🤗
entry.summary: I am happy to share the first post in the PGM Lab.
